risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The participants were randomised with a computer-generated randomisation list stratified by centre. ... The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete." Comment: This describes a clear random allocation method (computer-generated list) with stratification and block size, which are consistent with proper random sequence generation. The lack of reveal pertains to concealment, but for this domain the explicit computer-generated randomisation is sufficient for Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: The participants were randomised with a computer-generated randomisation list stratified by centre. ... The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete.
_evidence.method_summary: Computer-generated randomisation list stratified by centre, with block size 10 and concealed allocation code.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "At each centre, the person responsible for inclusion emailed the research unit, presented the participant’s project ID number, name and gender before treatment allocation was disclosed. The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete." Comment: This describes a central allocation mechanism in which the recruiter could not know the upcoming assignment before enrollment, so allocation was adequately concealed.

_evidence.concealment_quote: At each centre, the person responsible for inclusion emailed the research unit, presented the participant’s project ID number, name and gender before treatment allocation was disclosed. The allocation code, including details of block size (10), was not revealed to the researchers or the clinicians until recruitment and data collection were complete.
_evidence.note: Sequence generation is described (computer-generated randomisation list stratified by centre), and concealment is described via disclosure only through the research unit; no envelopes are mentioned.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "After random allocation, participants were informed about the outcome, and the intervention group was given a date for its first session." Comment: The article does not describe blinding of participants or the personnel delivering the intervention, and the intervention itself was necessarily apparent to both groups (AWaC sessions vs standard treatment). Because the study reports subjective patient-reported outcomes (HAD total, depression, anxiety, EQ5D) in addition to work outcomes, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence those outcomes; therefore the dominant risk for this domain is high.

_evidence.blinding_quote: The authors in charge of the data analysis were blinded for intervention assignment.
_evidence.blinding_method: none described for participants or personnel; the intervention group was given a date for its first session and the control group received standard treatment

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The authors in charge of the data analysis were blinded for intervention assignment." Comment: This indicates the data analysts were blinded, but the outcome assessment itself was mixed. The primary work-participation outcome came from registry data and is objective, yet the study also reports patient-reported secondary outcomes: "Secondary outcome measures were questionnaire-based changes in psychological distress, and symptoms of anxiety and depression by use of the Hospital Anxiety and Depression (HAD) Scale. EQ5D was used to measure changes in health-related quality of life. Self-report questionnaires were administered to the patients" and Table 3 reports these outcomes. Because the patient is the assessor for these self-report measures, and participants were not reported as blinded, knowledge of allocation could bias measurement of these subjective outcomes. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes the overall domain High risk even though the registry-based primary outcome was objective.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: The authors in charge of the data analysis were blinded for intervention assignment.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Data on the main outcome measure, work participation, were complete for all participants." Comment: The primary analysed outcome at 12 months had complete follow-up in both arms (AWaC 630/630 analysed; Control 563/563 analysed; 0% loss, no arm imbalance), so attrition could not have biased the main result. The reported non-response and inverse probability weighting applied only to secondary self-report outcomes, not to the primary work-participation outcome.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “Data on the main outcome measure, work participation, were complete for all participants.”
“Data on the main outcome measure, work participation, were complete for all participants.”

_evidence.missing_data_method: intention-to-treat; inverse probability weights for secondary outcomes
_evidence.notes: Primary outcome at 12 months had complete registry data with no loss to follow-up. Non-response affected secondary self-report outcomes only (e.g., 740/1193 at 6 months; 636/1193 at 12 months), which were analyzed with inverse probability weights.
